From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

State-maintained and numbered road

"State road" redirects here. For the unincorporated area in Delaware, see [State Road, Delaware](/wiki/State_Road,_Delaware "State Road, Delaware").

Several terms redirect here. For other uses, see [Territorial Road (Michigan)](/wiki/Territorial_Road_(Michigan) "Territorial Road (Michigan)"), [New Jersey Route 139](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_139 "New Jersey Route 139"), and [Oregon Route 200](/wiki/Oregon_Route_200 "Oregon Route 200").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Amalfitana_13.jpg/250px-Amalfitana_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amalfitana_13.jpg)

[Strada statale 163 Amalfitana](/wiki/Strada_statale_163_Amalfitana "Strada statale 163 Amalfitana") in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), which runs along the stretch of the [Amalfi Coast](/wiki/Amalfi_Coast "Amalfi Coast") between the [southern Italian](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") towns of [Sorrento](/wiki/Sorrento "Sorrento") and [Amalfi](/wiki/Amalfi "Amalfi").

A **state highway**, **state road**, or **state route** (and the equivalent **provincial highway**, **provincial road**, or **provincial route**) is usually a [road](/wiki/Road "Road") that is either [numbered](/wiki/Route_number "Route number") or maintained by a sub-national state or [province](/wiki/Province "Province"). A road numbered by a state or province falls below numbered national highways ([Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") being [a notable exception](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway#Jurisdiction_and_designation "Trans-Canada Highway") to this rule) in the hierarchy (route numbers are used to aid navigation, and may or may not indicate ownership or maintenance).

Roads maintained by a state or province include both nationally numbered highways and un-numbered state highways. Depending on the state, "state highway" may be used for one meaning and "state road" or "state route" for the other.

In some countries such as [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), the word "state" is used in its sense of a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") or country. By this meaning a state highway is a road maintained and numbered by the national government rather than local authorities.

## By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: By country")]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Australia")]

Main article: [Route number § Australia](/wiki/Route_number#Australia "Route number")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Australian_state_route_3.svg/120px-Australian_state_route_3.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Australian_state_route_3.svg)

Australian State Route 3 marker

[Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia")'s important urban and inter-regional routes not covered by the National Highway or National Route systems are marked under the State Route system. They can be recognised by blue shield markers. They were practically adopted in all states by the end of the 1980s, and in some states, some less important National Routes were downgraded to State Routes. Each state has or had its own numbering scheme, but do not duplicate National Route numbers in the same state, or nearby routes in another state.[[1]](#cite_note-NAASRA-1)

As with the National Routes and National Highways, State Routes are being phased out in most states and territories in favour of alphanumeric routes.[[2]](#cite_note-RMS-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Queensland-3)[[4]](#cite_note-MRWA_signs-4)[[5]](#cite_note-RMS_alpha_QandA-5) However, despite the fact that Victoria has fully adopted alphanumeric routes in regional areas, state route numbers are still used extensively within the city of Melbourne as a part of its [Metropolitan Route Numbering Scheme](/wiki/List_of_old_road_routes_in_Victoria "List of old road routes in Victoria").[[6]](#cite_note-VicRoads_signs-6)

Unlike in other Australian states, State Routes in Western Australia carry a large importance by representing major routes across the state, as Western Australia has not adopted the Alphanumeric numbering system.

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Brazil")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=). *(May 2024)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/SP-270.png/120px-SP-270.png)](/wiki/File:SP-270.png)

[SP-270](/wiki/Rodovia_Raposo_Tavares "Rodovia Raposo Tavares") state highway shield in the state of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)")

[Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") is another country that is divided into states and has state highways. For example, the longest highway in the state of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), the [Rodovia Raposo Tavares](/wiki/Rodovia_Raposo_Tavares "Rodovia Raposo Tavares"), is designated as [SP-270 and SP-295](/wiki/Rodovia_Raposo_Tavares "Rodovia Raposo Tavares").

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Ontario_401.svg/120px-Ontario_401.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ontario_401.svg)

An [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") provincial highway marker

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/ON_400_TCH.jpg/250px-ON_400_TCH.jpg)](/wiki/File:ON_400_TCH.jpg)

A Canadian provincial highway double-signed with a numberless, but named, national Trans-Canada marker

Main article: [Numbered highways in Canada](/wiki/Numbered_highways_in_Canada "Numbered highways in Canada")

[Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") is divided into provinces and territories, each of which maintains its own system of provincial or territorial highways, which form the majority of the country's highway network. There is also the national transcontinental [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway") system, which is marked by distinct signs, but has no uniform numeric designation across the country. In the eastern provinces, for instance, an unnumbered (though sometimes with a named route branch) Trans-Canada route marker is co-signed with a numbered provincial sign, with the provincial route often continuing alone outside the Trans-Canada Highway section. However, in the western provinces, the two parallel Trans-Canada routes are consistently numbered with Trans-Canada route markers; as Highways 1 and 16 respectively.

Canada also has a designated [National Highway System](/wiki/National_Highway_System_(Canada) "National Highway System (Canada)"), but the system is completely unsigned, aside from the Trans-Canada routes. This makes Canada unique in that national highway designations are generally secondary to subnational routes.

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Germany")]

Main article: [Landesstraße](/wiki/Landesstra%C3%9Fe "Landesstraße")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/B98-neu-bei-Neustadt.jpg/250px-B98-neu-bei-Neustadt.jpg)](/wiki/File:B98-neu-bei-Neustadt.jpg)

*Staatsstrasse* 156 in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony")

In [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), state roads (**Landesstraßen** or *Staatsstraßen*) are a road class which is ranking below the federal road network (*Bundesstraßen*). The responsibility for road planning, construction and maintenance is vested in the federal states of Germany.

Most federal states use the term *Landesstraße* (marked with 'L'), while for historical reasons [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") and [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") use the term *Staatsstraße* (marked with 'S'). The appearance of the shields differs from state to state.

The term *Land-es-straße* should not be confused with *Landstraße*, which describes every road outside built-up areas and is not a road class.

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Italy")]

Main article: [State highway (Italy)](/wiki/State_highway_(Italy) "State highway (Italy)")

See also: [Roads in Italy](/wiki/Roads_in_Italy "Roads in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Strada_Statale_1_Italia.svg/250px-Strada_Statale_1_Italia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Strada_Statale_1_Italia.svg)

Road marker for state highways in Italy

The [Strade Statali](/wiki/State_highways_(Italy) "State highways (Italy)"), abbreviated SS, is the [Italian](/wiki/Italy "Italy") national network of state highways. The total length for the network is about 25.000 km (15.534 mi).[[7]](#cite_note-stradeanas.it-7) The Italian state highway network are maintained by [ANAS](/wiki/ANAS "ANAS"). From 1928 until 1946 state highways were maintained by Azienda Autonoma Statale della Strada (AASS). The next level of roads below Strada Statali is [Strada Regionale](/wiki/Regional_road_(Italy) "Regional road (Italy)") ("regional roads"). The routes of some state highways derive from ancient [Roman roads](/wiki/Roman_roads "Roman roads"), such as the [Strada statale 7 Via Appia](/wiki/Strada_statale_7_Via_Appia "Strada statale 7 Via Appia"), which broadly follows the route of the [Roman road of the same name](/wiki/Appian_Way "Appian Way"). Other examples are the [Strada statale 1 Via Aurelia](/wiki/Strada_statale_1_Via_Aurelia "Strada statale 1 Via Aurelia") ([Via Aurelia](/wiki/Via_Aurelia "Via Aurelia")) and the [Strada statale 4 Via Salaria](/wiki/Strada_statale_4_Via_Salaria "Strada statale 4 Via Salaria") ([Via Salaria](/wiki/Via_Salaria "Via Salaria")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Arenzano-IMG_0632.JPG/250px-Arenzano-IMG_0632.JPG)](/wiki/File:Arenzano-IMG_0632.JPG)

[Strada statale 1 Via Aurelia](/wiki/Strada_statale_1_Via_Aurelia "Strada statale 1 Via Aurelia") in [Arenzano](/wiki/Arenzano "Arenzano"). It is one of the most important state highways in Italy and derives from an ancient Roman consular road, the [Via Aurelia](/wiki/Via_Aurelia "Via Aurelia").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Soraga_001.JPG/250px-Soraga_001.JPG)](/wiki/File:Soraga_001.JPG)

[Strada statale 48 delle Dolomiti](/wiki/Strada_statale_48_delle_Dolomiti "Strada statale 48 delle Dolomiti") in [Soraga](/wiki/Soraga_di_Fassa "Soraga di Fassa"). It is a mountainous road that runs through the [Dolomites](/wiki/Dolomites "Dolomites") area from [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), through [Trentino](/wiki/Trentino "Trentino") to [Cadore](/wiki/Cadore "Cadore").

Since the reforms following the [birth of the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy") in 1861, the State took charge of the construction and maintenance of a primary network of roads for connections between the main cities; in 1865 the [Lanza](/wiki/Giovanni_Lanza "Giovanni Lanza") law introduced the classification of roads between national, provincial and municipal (see Annex F, art.10)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) and the Royal Decree of 17 November 1865, n. 2633[[9]](#cite_note-9) listed the first 38 national roads.

Italian state highways are identified by a number and a name. In road signs and maps the number is preceded by the acronym SS, an acronym for *strada statale* ("state road"). The nomenclature of the state highways managed by [ANAS](/wiki/ANAS "ANAS") generally follows the SS *n* scheme, where *n* is a number ranging from 1 ([Aurelia](/wiki/Strada_statale_1_Via_Aurelia "Strada statale 1 Via Aurelia")) up to 700 (of the [Royal Palace of Caserta](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Caserta "Royal Palace of Caserta")) depending on the date of establishment of the state highway. Newly built ANAS roads, not yet classified, are identified by the acronym NSA, an acronym for *nuova strada ANAS* ("new ANAS road").[[7]](#cite_note-stradeanas.it-7)

State highways can be technically defined as main extra-urban roads (type B road) or as secondary extra-urban roads (type C road). State highways that cross towns with a population of at least 10,000 inhabitants are urban roads (type D and E) under the jurisdiction of the relevant municipalities. The state highway that cross towns or villages with a population of less than 10,000 inhabitants are urban roads (type D and E) under the jurisdiction of the municipality, subject to authorization from [ANAS](/wiki/ANAS "ANAS").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Notable state highways in Italy are the [Strada statale 1 Via Aurelia](/wiki/Strada_statale_1_Via_Aurelia "Strada statale 1 Via Aurelia"), the [Strada statale 4 Via Salaria](/wiki/Strada_statale_4_Via_Salaria "Strada statale 4 Via Salaria"), the [Strada statale 4 dir](/wiki/Strada_statale_4_dir "Strada statale 4 dir"), [Strada statale 7 Via Appia](/wiki/Strada_statale_7_Via_Appia "Strada statale 7 Via Appia"), the [Strada statale 12 dell'Abetone e del Brennero](/wiki/Strada_statale_12_dell%27Abetone_e_del_Brennero "Strada statale 12 dell'Abetone e del Brennero"), the [Strada statale 18 Tirrena Inferiore](/wiki/Strada_statale_18_Tirrena_Inferiore "Strada statale 18 Tirrena Inferiore"), the [Strada statale 35 dei Giovi](/wiki/Strada_statale_35_dei_Giovi "Strada statale 35 dei Giovi"), the [Strada statale 36 del Lago di Como e dello Spluga](/wiki/Strada_statale_36_del_Lago_di_Como_e_dello_Spluga "Strada statale 36 del Lago di Como e dello Spluga"), the [Strada statale 37 del Maloja](/wiki/Strada_statale_37_del_Maloja "Strada statale 37 del Maloja"), the [Strada statale 38 dello Stelvio](/wiki/Strada_statale_38_dello_Stelvio "Strada statale 38 dello Stelvio"), the [Strada statale 44 del Passo di Giovo](/wiki/Strada_statale_44_del_Passo_di_Giovo "Strada statale 44 del Passo di Giovo"), the [Strada statale 44 bis Passo del Rombo](/wiki/Strada_statale_44_bis_Passo_del_Rombo "Strada statale 44 bis Passo del Rombo"), the [Strada statale 48 delle Dolomiti](/wiki/Strada_statale_48_delle_Dolomiti "Strada statale 48 delle Dolomiti"), the [Strada statale 72 di San Marino](/wiki/Strada_statale_72_di_San_Marino "Strada statale 72 di San Marino"), the [Strada statale 106 Jonica](/wiki/Strada_statale_106_Jonica "Strada statale 106 Jonica"), the [Strada statale 115 Sud Occidentale Sicula](/wiki/Strada_statale_115_Sud_Occidentale_Sicula "Strada statale 115 Sud Occidentale Sicula"), the [Strada statale 125 Orientale Sarda](/wiki/Strada_statale_125_Orientale_Sarda "Strada statale 125 Orientale Sarda"), the [Strada statale 131 Carlo Felice](/wiki/Strada_statale_131_Carlo_Felice "Strada statale 131 Carlo Felice"), the [Strada statale 131 Diramazione Centrale Nuorese](/wiki/Strada_statale_131_Diramazione_Centrale_Nuorese "Strada statale 131 Diramazione Centrale Nuorese"), the [Strada statale 148 Pontina](/wiki/Strada_statale_148_Pontina "Strada statale 148 Pontina"), the [Strada statale 163 Amalfitana](/wiki/Strada_statale_163_Amalfitana "Strada statale 163 Amalfitana"), the [Strada statale 336 dell'Aeroporto della Malpensa](/wiki/Strada_statale_336_dell%27Aeroporto_della_Malpensa "Strada statale 336 dell'Aeroporto della Malpensa") and the [Strada statale 407 Basentana](/wiki/Strada_statale_407_Basentana "Strada statale 407 Basentana").

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: India")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Road_marker_IN_SH.svg/120px-Road_marker_IN_SH.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Road_marker_IN_SH.svg)

Road marker for state highways in India

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Road_marker_IN_SH-U.svg/120px-Road_marker_IN_SH-U.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Road_marker_IN_SH-U.svg)

Road marker for state highways in urban stretches of India

[State highways in India](/wiki/State_highways_in_India "State highways in India") are numbered highways that are laid and maintained by [state governments](/wiki/State_governments_of_India "State governments of India").

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [Prefectural road](/wiki/Prefectural_road "Prefectural road")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Fukuoka_Pref_Route_Sign_0008.svg/120px-Fukuoka_Pref_Route_Sign_0008.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Fukuoka_Pref_Route_Sign_0008.svg)

Road marker for Fukuoka Prefectural Route 8

In Japan, prefectural roads are maintained by the governments of the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan") they are in. By length, 10.7% of public roads in Japan were prefectural roads as of 2011; by usage, they carried more than 30% of all traffic volume on public roads as of 2007.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mexico")]

[Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico")'s [State Highway System](/wiki/Numbered_highways_in_Mexico "Numbered highways in Mexico") is a system of urban and state routes constructed and maintained by each Mexican state. The main purpose of the state networks is to serve as a feeder system to the federal highway system. All states except the [Federal District](/wiki/Mexican_Federal_District "Mexican Federal District") operate a road network. Each state marks these routes with a white shield containing the abbreviated name of the state plus the route number.

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Main article: [New Zealand state highway network](/wiki/New_Zealand_state_highway_network "New Zealand state highway network")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/State_Highway_1_NZ.svg/120px-State_Highway_1_NZ.svg.png)](/wiki/File:State_Highway_1_NZ.svg)

New Zealand state highway marker

New Zealand state highways are national highways – the word "state" in this sense means "government" or "public" (as in [state housing](/wiki/State_housing_in_New_Zealand "State housing in New Zealand") and [state schools](/wiki/State_school "State school")), not a division of a country.

New Zealand's state highway system is a nationwide network of roads covering the [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island") and the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island"). As of 2006, just under 100 roads have a "State Highway" designation. The [NZ Transport Agency](/wiki/NZ_Transport_Agency "NZ Transport Agency") administers them. The speed limit for most state highways is 100 km/h, with reductions when one passes through a densely populated area.

The highways in New Zealand are all state highways, and the network consists of SH 1 running the length of both main islands, SH 2–5 and 10–59 in the North Island, and SH 6–8 and 60–99 in the South Island. National and provincial highways are numbered approximately north to south. [State Highway 1](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_1 "New Zealand State Highway 1") runs the length of both islands.

### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: South Korea")]

Main article: [Local highways of South Korea](/wiki/Local_highways_of_South_Korea "Local highways of South Korea")

In South Korea, local highways ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): 지방도; [Hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja"): 地方道; [RR](/wiki/Revised_Romanization_of_Korean "Revised Romanization of Korean"): *Jibangdo*; [MR](/wiki/McCune%E2%80%93Reischauer "McCune–Reischauer"): *Chipangdo*) are the next important roads under the [national highways](/wiki/National_highways_of_South_Korea "National highways of South Korea"). The number has two, three, or four digits. Highways with two-digit numbers are called state-funded local highways.

### Turkey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Turkey")]

Main article: [Turkish State Highway System](/wiki/Turkish_State_Highway_System "Turkish State Highway System")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/D100-TR.svg/250px-D100-TR.svg.png)](/wiki/File:D100-TR.svg)

Highway shield of the [D.100](/wiki/D.100 "D.100"), a major state highway in Turkey.

In Turkey, state roads ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *devlet yolu*) are primary roads, mostly under the responsibility of [General Directorate of Highways](/wiki/General_Directorate_of_Highways_(Turkey) "General Directorate of Highways (Turkey)") (KGM) except in [metropolitan city centers](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipalities_in_Turkey "Metropolitan municipalities in Turkey") where the responsibility lies with local government. The roads have a three-digit number designation, preceded by *D*.

Provincial roads ([Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"): *İl yolu*) are secondary roads, maintained by respective local governments with the support of the KGM. The roads have a four-digit numbering grouped as two pairs separated by a dash. The first pair represents the [license number of that province](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Turkey "Vehicle registration plates of Turkey").

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_highway&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: United States")]

See also: [Numbered highways in the United States § State highways](/wiki/Numbered_highways_in_the_United_States#State_highways "Numbered highways in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Circle_sign_50.svg/120px-Circle_sign_50.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Circle_sign_50.svg)

Default U.S. state highway marker

In the United States, state highways are generally a mixture of primary and secondary roads, although some are [freeways](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway") (for example, [State Route 99](/wiki/California_State_Route_99 "California State Route 99") in California, which links many of the cities of the [Central Valley](/wiki/Central_Valley_(California) "Central Valley (California)"), [Route 128](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_128 "Massachusetts Route 128") in Massachusetts, or parts of [Route 101](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Route_101 "New Hampshire Route 101") in New Hampshire). Each state has its own system for numbering and its own marker. The default marker is a [white circle containing a black sans-serif number](/wiki/Circular_highway_shield "Circular highway shield") (often inscribed in a black square or slightly rounded square), according to the *[Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices](/wiki/Manual_on_Uniform_Traffic_Control_Devices "Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices")* (MUTCD). However each state is free to choose a different marker,[[12]](#cite_note-12) and most states have. States may choose a design theme relevant to its state (such as an outline of the state itself) to distinguish state route markers from interstate, county, or municipal route markers. The longest state highway in the U.S. is [Montana Highway 200](/wiki/Montana_Highway_200 "Montana Highway 200").
